problem_summary: 2018 MCM/ICM Problem B (Outstanding, Team 91566): "Forecasting the Language Distribution." Part I asks for (A) a model of how the number of speakers of each language evolves over time under impact factors, (B) a 50-year forecast of total speaker counts, and (C) a 50-year forecast of the GEOGRAPHIC distribution of speakers across countries. Part II asks (A) where a multinational service company should open new international offices given the predicted language landscape and economics, and (B) how that recommendation should evolve over the long term. The team built a nested family of dynamical models culminating in a country-by-country, language-by-language cellular-automaton simulation, then layered a marginal-benefit hiring/office-location model on top.
models_used: Worldwide Language Shift Model: a multi-language generalization of the Abrams-Strogatz (A-S) language-death ODE, extended from 2 languages to 26. Core ODE: dQ_l/dt = sum over l' of [Q_l' * P_{l'->l}] - sum over l' of [Q_l * P_{l->l'}] + G*Q_l*(1 - P_t/K) (Eq 9), i.e. gains from in-shift, losses to out-shift, plus a logistic population-growth term with global carrying capacity K=1.1e10
Pinasco-Romanelli-style logistic natural-reproduction term embedded in the shift ODE (the G*Q*(1-P/K) carrying-capacity term)

Parameterized shift-probability function P_{l'->l} = c * s_{l,t} * (Q_{l,t}/P_t)^{a_{l,t}} (Eq 10), generalizing the A-S c*n^a*s form, where the exponent a (resistance) and status s are themselves regression-style functions of real covariates
Resistance-level sub-model: a_{l,t} as a linear function of tourism share, import/export (trade) share, a linear technology-time trend (psi*t), and Facebook language-group membership D_l (Eq 11)
Language-status sub-model: s_{l,t} = R_{l,t}*zeta + s_bar, where R is the fraction of countries using the language officially (Eq 12)

Domestic Language Shift Model: the same ODE re-instantiated per country i with per-country carrying capacity K_i (assumed 1.5x 2016 population) and an official-language indicator R_{i,l,t} in {0,1} (Eqs 13-20)
Migration Model: net bilateral migration rates V_ij estimated from UN 5-year migrant-stock data 1991-2017, averaged to V_bar_ij, then used to move speakers between countries (Eqs 21-26)

Geographic Language Shift Model: superposition of the Domestic shift model + the Migration model into a single discrete-time update (Eq 27), simulated as a Cellular Automaton over (country x language x year) cells to roll the global state forward 50 years

Parameter fitting via gradient descent minimizing sum-of-squared-errors between predicted and actual yearly changes 2014-2017 (Algorithm 1), yielding ~9 fitted parameters (G,c,phi,rho,psi,a_bar,zeta,delta,s_bar)

Office-location / hiring model: a marginal economic-benefit optimization. Net benefit Lambda(n) = Q(n)/P_{i,t} * F - C*n; optimal number of languages n* found where marginal speaker coverage equals cost ratio C/F: [Q(n)-Q(n-1)]/P_{i,t} = C/F (Eqs 29-30)
One-way ANOVA used as the verification instrument: comparing per-language and per-country 2018 vs 2068 distributions (F/P-values) to argue distributions are statistically stable
GaWC (Globalization and World Cities Research Network) city-tier ranking used as the tie-breaker to pick specific cities among economically-qualified countries
key_techniques: Model laddering: start from the textbook A-S 2-language model, then build up in clearly-labeled stages (Worldwide -> Domestic -> Migration -> Geographic) so each added complexity is motivated and traceable

Embedding real, collectible covariates into abstract A-S parameters: resistance a and status s are not free knobs but linear functions of tourism, trade (import/export), official-language ratio, Facebook group membership, and a technology time-trend - this is what made the model 'fittable' to real data

Clever proxy construction for hard-to-measure quantities: language-weighted tourism T_l = sum_i T_i * omega_{i,l} and language-weighted trade tiev_l via the per-country speaker-share weights omega_{i,l}; Facebook 'study group' member counts as a social-media popularity proxy

Reducing per-country time-series gaps to constants: tourism/GDP ratios Gamma and Omega computed as 17-year historical means (2000-2016), so the model needs only cross-section 2016 data plus a few years of speaker counts

Cellular-automaton discretization of the coupled ODE+migration system so the whole world (countries x languages) self-evolves year by year from an initial 2016 state - 'the model runs spontaneously after it is established'
Using one-way ANOVA both as a results-presentation device (2018 vs 2068 are not significantly different -> 'basically stable') AND as a sensitivity/robustness test

Robustness test by adversarial data perturbation: deliberately swapping/corrupting one random country's language counts (20 trials) and re-running ANOVA to show a single country's data error (e.g. the bad Malay data) does not move the global result

Translating the business sub-problem into a marginal-analysis calculus problem: set dLambda/dn = 0 to derive a clean decision rule (recruit until marginal speaker coverage drops below C/F), then filter cities by GaWC tier
Memo-to-COO framing: a standalone one-page non-technical letter to the Chief Operating Officer up front, satisfying the ICM communication requirement
why_it_won: "It directly and completely answered every sub-part (I-A/B/C, II-A/B) with one coherent, escalating model family rather than disconnected pieces, and the escalation was legible to a judge: A-S baseline -> multi-language worldwide -> per-country -> +migration -> geographic CA. Crucially it closed the modeling-to-data loop that most teams leave open - the A-S resistance/status parameters were re-expressed as functions of genuinely collectible quantities (Ethnologue speaker counts, UN migration stocks, World Bank trade/GDP, departures/tourism, Facebook group sizes), then actually fit by gradient descent on 2014-2017 deltas, producing concrete numeric parameter estimates. The cellular-automaton realization let a single mechanism answer the global, national, and community-level questions simultaneously, which reads as elegance. It backed its headline claim ('distribution basically stable for 50 years') with quantitative ANOVA evidence rather than assertion, and stress-tested that claim three ways (K_i, single-country data corruption, fitting parameters a/s/c). Finally it nailed ICM communication: a self-contained COO memo, an explicit C/F marginal-benefit decision rule yielding six named cities with the exact languages each office needs, and an honest long-term caveat that technology erodes language advantage so fewer than six offices may suffice later."
section_highlights: "Strong structural choices: (1) A front-loaded one-page memo 'To: Chief Operating Officer' that states the answer (six cities + languages) before any math - exactly the ICM deliverable. (2) A 'Related Work' subsection (1.3) that derives the A-S model and the Pinasco-Romanelli reproduction extension explicitly, so the team's own model reads as a principled extension of cited literature, not an invention from nowhere. (3) Each new model layer opens with a 'Factors' subsection in plain language (government support, tourism, business, technology / migration) before equations, giving the judge the intuition first. (4) Numbered 'Algorithm 1: Parameter Fitting' as an explicit 4-step recipe. (5) A clean Notations table and Assumptions section that justifies the scope reductions (26 languages >=50M speakers, 36 top population/GDP countries) with reasons. (6) Results mapped one-to-one onto the problem's labeled sub-parts (4.1 Part I Problem A ... 4.5 Part II Problem B), so a grader can check coverage at a glance. (7) Dedicated Sensitivity Analysis section with three distinct experiments, and an honest Strengths/Weaknesses/Further-work section."
pitfalls_or_limitations: Thin calibration data: only 4 years of speaker counts (2014-2017) used to fit ~9 nonlinear parameters via gradient descent - high overfitting risk and weak basis for a 50-year extrapolation; the team concedes long fit times and heavy manual derivation

Methodological circularity in the central claim: the model is essentially near-equilibrium by construction (status driven by slow-changing official-language ratios, resistance with a small linear tech trend), so concluding 'distribution stays stable for 50 years' partly reflects the model's own inertia rather than an independent finding

ANOVA is arguably the wrong test for the stability question: a non-significant F merely fails to reject equal means across two snapshots; it is not evidence of equivalence, and applying it to deterministic model outputs (no real sampling noise) is statistically shaky

Strong simplifying assumptions left unexamined: a single shared maximum shift rate c across all language pairs (admitted weakness), K_i = 1.5x present population, migration rates frozen at the 1991-2017 average for 50 years, omega_{i,l} treated as constant

Proxy validity is unverified: Facebook 'study group' membership as a social-media language-popularity signal, and equating a country's tourist/trade language mix with its overall speaker share, are convenient but unvalidated
Data quality holes acknowledged only after the fact: Malay fails ANOVA because it aggregates several languages; the team patches this with a robustness narrative rather than fixing the input

The Part II office model hard-codes C = F = 10% with no empirical basis, and the long-term answer (II-B) is left qualitative ('n may decrease, maybe fewer than 6 offices') because the needed cost/return data were never obtained

The rendered figures/charts in this corpus copy are garbled (placeholder repeated values, malformed world-map tables), so the quantitative forecasts cannot be independently audited from the document itself
reusable_patterns: Model-laddering template: open from a canonical published baseline (here A-S), then add one well-motivated mechanism per section (multi-class -> spatial/per-region -> coupling/migration -> unified simulation). Label each layer so escalating complexity stays auditable

Make abstract model parameters into regressions on collectible covariates: instead of leaving a 'resistance' or 'status' knob free, write it as a linear combination of real, citable data series, then fit - this converts a toy model into a data-grounded one and gives you a fitting story

Proxy-by-share-weighting: when you only have a per-country quantity X_i and per-country class shares omega_{i,c}, build a class-level quantity X_c = sum_i X_i * omega_{i,c}. Reusable for distributing tourism, trade, spend, emissions, etc. across categories
Collapse missing time-series into historical-mean constants (Gamma, Omega over 2000-2016) so a dynamic model can run on mostly cross-sectional data plus a short calibration window

Cellular-automaton / discrete-time superposition: realize coupled ODE dynamics + a transport/migration process as one yearly state-update over a (region x class) grid, so a single engine answers global, regional, and local questions at once

Use ANOVA (or any equivalence-style comparison) of t=0 vs t=horizon snapshots as a compact way to argue 'distribution is stable' - but pair it with the caveat above; better to add an explicit equivalence test or confidence band
Robustness-by-perturbation: inject controlled noise into the worst-quality input (swap/corrupt one unit's data, repeat N trials, re-test) to show the headline conclusion survives known data defects

Turn a 'where/how-many' business question into marginal analysis: define net benefit Lambda(n), set dLambda/dn = 0 to get a closed-form decision rule (marginal-coverage = cost/benefit ratio), then apply an external authority ranking (GaWC city tiers) as the tie-breaker to produce a concrete shortlist

Deliverable framing for ICM: lead with a self-contained one-page memo to the named stakeholder that states the decision and the per-option specifics before any equations, and end with an honest forward-looking caveat about what would change the recommendation
